perturbation.original_text: Section 1. Definition of Confidential Information . (a) The term “ Confidential Information ” also shall be deemed to include (i) any and all information concerning the Disclosing Party which has been or, in the future, is furnished by the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives to the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives, orally or in writing (whatever the form or storage medium), including, without limitation, information concerning the Disclosing Party’s business, operations, markets, products, services, designs, documentation, technical data or other proprietary information relating to actual, planned or possible products or services, trade secrets, events, processes, inventions (whether or not patentable), computer software and programs (in object or source code form), databases, original works of authorship, confidential knowledge, know -how, ideas, research and development, financial information, r esults of operations, projections, strategies, marketing information, contracts, employee information, business plans or other subject matter pertaining to the Disclosing Party and/or information regarding actual, planned or possible customers, clients, 2 consultants, contractors, advertisers, licensees, distributors, retailers and other business associates of the Disclosing Party and/or their respective businesses, operations, activities or plans , and ( ii) any and all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the Confidential Information described in clause (i) . (b) The term “ Confidential Information ” does not incl ude information which (i) is common knowledge with respect to companies operating in the industry in which the Disclosing Party conducts i ts business, (ii) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by the Receivin g Party or its Representatives to the public or any third party in violation of this Agreement, (iii) was rightfully within the Receiving Party’s or its Representatives’ possession prior to its being furnished to the Receiving Party by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party (as reasonably demonstrated by written evidence with respect thereto ), provided that the source of such information was not known by the Receiving Party to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiducia ry obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information, (iv) becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non -confidential basis from a source other than the Disclosing Party or any of its Representati ves, provided that such source is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiduciary obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information , (v) is independently develop ed by the Receiving Party or its Representatives without use of or reference to the Confidential Information or (v i) is required or requested to be disclosed by the Receiving Party by a governmental agency or law or other regulatory body as provided in Section 3(b) , so long as the Receiving Party complies with the requirements of such section.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 1. Definition of Confidential Information . (a) The term “ Confidential Information ” also shall be deemed to include (i) any and all information concerning the Disclosing Party which has been or, in the future, is furnished by the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives to the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives, orally or in writing (whatever the form or storage medium), including, without limitation, information concerning the Disclosing Party’s business, operations, markets, products, services, designs, documentation, technical data or other proprietary information relating to actual, planned or possible products or services, trade secrets, events, processes, inventions (whether or not patentable), computer software and programs (in object or source code form), databases, original works of authorship, confidential knowledge, know -how, ideas, research and development, financial information, r esults of operations, projections, strategies, marketing information, contracts, employee information, business plans or other subject matter pertaining to the Disclosing Party and/or information regarding actual, planned or possible customers, clients, 2 consultants, contractors, advertisers, licensees, distributors, retailers and other business associates of the Disclosing Party and/or their respective businesses, operations, activities or plans , and ( ii) any and all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the Confidential Information described in clause (i) . (b) The term “ Confidential Information ” does not include information which (i) is common knowledge with respect to companies operating in the industry in which the Disclosing Party conducts its business, (ii) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or its Representatives to the public or any third party in violation of this Agreement, (iii) was rightfully within the Receiving Party’s or its Representatives’ possession prior to its being furnished to the Receiving Party by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party (as reasonably demonstrated by written evidence with respect thereto ), provided that the source of such information was known by the Receiving Party to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiduciary obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information, (iv) becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non -confidential basis from a source other than the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, provided that such source is bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiduciary obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information , (v) is independently developed by the Receiving Party or its Representatives without use of or reference to the Confidential Information or (v i) is required or requested to be disclosed by the Receiving Party by a governmental agency or law or other regulatory body as provided in Section 3(b) , so long as the Receiving Party complies with the requirements of such section.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1(b)(iii) states that information rightfully in the Receiving Party's possession prior to disclosure is excluded from the definition of Confidential Information, provided the source wasn't known to be bound by a confidentiality agreement. The perturbation changes 'was not known' to 'was known'. This creates a contradiction in Section 1(b)(iii). Now, information rightfully in the Receiving Party's possession IS confidential even if the source was known to be bound by a confidentiality agreement, directly contradicting the original intent. This intext contradiction will create enforcement uncertainty on what really constitutes confidential information, specially in the case when the receiving party already had access to this type of information before the NDA being signed
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 1. Definition of Confidential Information . (a) The term “ Confidential Information ” also shall be deemed to include (i) any and all information concerning the Disclosing Party which has been or, in the future, is furnished by the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives to the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives, orally or in writing (whatever the form or storage medium), including, without limitation, information concerning the Disclosing Party’s business, operations, markets, products, services, designs, documentation, technical data or other proprietary information relating to actual, planned or possible products or services, trade secrets, events, processes, inventions (whether or not patentable), computer software and programs (in object or source code form), databases, original works of authorship, confidential knowledge, know -how, ideas, research and development, financial information, r esults of operations, projections, strategies, marketing information, contracts, employee information, business plans or other subject matter pertaining to the Disclosing Party and/or information regarding actual, planned or possible customers, clients, 2 consultants, contractors, advertisers, licensees, distributors, retailers and other business associates of the Disclosing Party and/or their respective businesses, operations, activities or plans , and ( ii) any and all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the Confidential Information described in clause (i) . (b) The term “ Confidential Information ” does not include information which (i) is common knowledge with respect to companies operating in the industry in which the Disclosing Party conducts i ts business, (ii) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by the Receivin g Party or its Representatives to the public or any third party in violation of this Agreement, (iii) was rightfully within the Receiving Party’s or its Representatives’ possession prior to its being furnished to the Receiving Party by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party (as reasonably demonstrated by written evidence with respect thereto ), provided that the source of such information was known by the Receiving Party to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiducia ry obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information, (iv) becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non -confidential basis from a source other than the Disclosing Party or any of its Representati ves, provided that such source is bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other contractual, legal or fiduciary obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party or any other party with respect to such information , (v) is independently develop ed by the Receiving Party or its Representatives without use of or reference to the Confidential Information or (v i) is required or requested to be disclosed by the Receiving Party by a governmental agency or law or other regulatory body as provided in Section 3(b) , so long as the Receiving Party complies with the requirements of such section.
perturbation.justification: The change in Section 1(b)(iii) from 'was not known' to 'was known' directly contradicts the original intent of excluding information already rightfully possessed by the Receiving Party. The contradicted text, which is actually the same text (Section 1), now includes a conflicting condition: previously possessed information is confidential even if the Receiving Party knew the source was bound by a confidentiality agreement. This creates a strong in-text contradiction regarding what constitutes Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5. Retu rn of Confidential Information ; Ownership . If either Party to this Agreement decides that it does not wish to proceed with a Possible Transaction with the other Party, such Party shall promptly inform the other Party of that decision. In that case, or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party for any reason, the Receiving Party shall promptly deliver to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information (and all copies thereo f) furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party and shall continue to maintain the confidentiality of all other Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives under the ter ms of this Agreement. Upon request by the 4 Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall confirm in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information (or continues to maintain the confidentiality of that Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives) and all copies thereof, in its possession. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain copies of Confidential Information stored in standard archival or computer bac k-up systems or retained pursuant to such Person’s normal document retention practices, for litigation and regulatory purposes or to the extent required by applicable law, provided that all such information retained by the Receiving Party or its Representa tives shall continue to be held subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. All Confidential Information shall remain at all times the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall acquire no rights in or to such Confidential Information by reason of its disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5. Retu rn of Confidential Information ; Ownership . If either Party to this Agreement decides that it does not wish to proceed with a Possible Transaction with the other Party, such Party shall promptly inform the other Party of that decision. In that case, or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party for any reason, the Receiving Party shall promptly deliver to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information (and all copies thereo f) furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party and shall continue to maintain the confidentiality of all other Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives under the ter ms of this Agreement. Upon request by the 4 Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall confirm in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information (or continues to maintain the confidentiality of that Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives) and all copies thereof, in its possession. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain copies of Confidential Information stored in standard archival or computer bac k-up systems or retained pursuant to such Person’s normal document retention practices, for litigation and regulatory purposes or to the extent required by applicable law, provided that all such information retained by the Receiving Party or its Representa tives shall cease to be held subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. All Confidential Information shall remain at all times the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall acquire no rights in or to such Confidential Information by reason of its disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5 originally states that retained copies of Confidential Information continue to be held subject to the terms and conditions of the Agreement. The perturbation changes 'continue to be held subject' to 'cease to be held subject'. This creates a contradiction within Section 5 itself. The first part of section 5 states that any returned or not destroyed 'confidential information' is still subject to the terms of this agreement, while the last parrt states that the that type of information that's in the posession of the receiver is no longer subject to the terms of the agreement. This intext contradiction will create enforcement uncertainty on what really are the obligations of the receiver regarding such information after a failed business transaction between the parties
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 5. Retu rn of Confidential Information ; Ownership . If either Party to this Agreement decides that it does not wish to proceed with a Possible Transaction with the other Party, such Party shall promptly inform the other Party of that decision. In that case, or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party for any reason, the Receiving Party shall promptly deliver to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information (and all copies thereo f) furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party and shall continue to maintain the confidentiality of all other Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives under the ter ms of this Agreement. Upon request by the 4 Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall confirm in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information (or continues to maintain the confidentiality of that Confidential Information prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives) and all copies thereof, in its possession. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain copies of Confidential Information stored in standard archival or computer bac k-up systems or retained pursuant to such Person’s normal document retention practices, for litigation and regulatory purposes or to the extent required by applicable law, provided that all such information retained by the Receiving Party or its Representa tives shall cease to be held subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. All Confidential Information shall remain at all times the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party shall acquire no rights in or to such Confidential Information by reason of its disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters Section 5, stating that retained copies of Confidential Information 'cease to be held subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement,' which directly contradicts the beginning of Section 5. The contradiction is strong because it affects the fundamental obligations regarding confidential information, creating uncertainty about what terms are enforceable, leading to a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 2. Use of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives shall (a) use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, (b) hold the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in trust and keep it confidential, (c) use the same means they use to protect their own confidential information, but in any event not less than reasonable means, to prevent the disclosure by them and any of th eir Representatives of the Confidential Information communicated to them by the Disclosing Party and its Representatives, and (d) not copy or reproduce, or permit to be copied or reproduced, in any way, any part of the Confidential Information of the Discl osing Party except in accordance with and for the purposes set forth in this Agreement. The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; prov ided, that any of such information may be disclosed by the Receiving Party to its Representatives who have a legitimate need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction with the Disclosing Party and who agree to keep such information confidential. In any event, the Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives , and each Party agrees, at its sole expense, to take commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Repr esentatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2. Use of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives shall (a) use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, (b) hold the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in trust and keep it confidential, (c) use the same means they use to protect their own confidential information, but in any event no means necessary, to prevent the disclosure by them and any of th eir Representatives of the Confidential Information communicated to them by the Disclosing Party and its Representatives, and (d) not copy or reproduce, or permit to be copied or reproduced, in any way, any part of the Confidential Information of the Discl osing Party except in accordance with and for the purposes set forth in this Agreement. The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; prov ided, that any of such information may be disclosed by the Receiving Party to its Representatives who have a legitimate need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction with the Disclosing Party and who agree to keep such information confidential. In any event, the Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives , and each Party agrees, at its sole expense, to take commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Repr esentatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.explanation: Section 2(c) obligates the Receiving Party to use at least reasonable means to prevent disclosure of Confidential Information. The perturbation changes 'not less than reasonable means' to 'no means necessary'. This creates a direct in-text contradiction within Section 2. By stating 'no means necessary,' the contract now implies that the Receiving Party has no obligation to actively prevent disclosure, directly contradicting the overall intent of the NDA and the previous clause about 'reasonable means'. This contradiction would create enforcement uncertainty since it is unclear whether the receiver should take actions or not to protect the information
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2. Use of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives shall (a) use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, (b) hold the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in trust and keep it confidential, (c) use the same means they use to protect their own confidential information, but in any event no means necessary, to prevent the disclosure by them and any of th eir Representatives of the Confidential Information communicated to them by the Disclosing Party and its Representatives, and (d) not copy or reproduce, or permit to be copied or reproduced, in any way, any part of the Confidential Information of the Discl osing Party except in accordance with and for the purposes set forth in this Agreement. The Receiving Party hereby agrees that the Receiving Party and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; prov ided, that any of such information may be disclosed by the Receiving Party to its Representatives who have a legitimate need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction with the Disclosing Party and who agree to keep such information confidential. In any event, the Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives , and each Party agrees, at its sole expense, to take commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Repr esentatives from prohibited or unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Receiving Party to use 'not less than reasonable means' to protect confidential information. The changed text modifies this to 'no means necessary', directly contradicting the initial obligation to protect confidential information and creating an in-text contradiction within Section 2. The contradicted text is the same section of the contract, Section 2, highlighting the in-text nature of the contradiction as the changes introduces the contradiction within the same section. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, where a clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications and creates uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7. Communications to Disclosing Party ; Notification . The Receiving Party agrees not to initiate or maintain contact (except for those contacts made in the ordinary course of business) with any officer, director, manager, partner , member , shareholder, employee or agent of the Disclosing Party regarding its business, operations, prospects or finances, except with the express permission of the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 . It is understood that the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 will arrange for appropriate contacts for due diligence purposes. Each Party agrees to give the other Party prompt notice of any violation of this A greement, including, without limitation, any unauthorized disclosure or use of the other Party’s Confidential Information .
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7. Communications to Disclosing Party ; Notification . The Receiving Party agrees to initiate or maintain contact (except for those contacts made in the ordinary course of business) with any officer, director, manager, partner , member , shareholder, employee or agent of the Disclosing Party regarding its business, operations, prospects or finances, except with the express permission of the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 . It is understood that the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 will arrange for appropriate contacts for due diligence purposes. Each Party agrees to give the other Party prompt notice of any violation of this A greement, including, without limitation, any unauthorized disclosure or use of the other Party’s Confidential Information .
perturbation.explanation: Section 7 restricts the Receiving Party from initiating contact with the Disclosing Party's personnel. The perturbation removes the word 'not,' so now the Receiving Party agrees TO initiate contact. This directly in-text contradicts the original intent of the section, which was to limit unauthorized communication channels and protect the Disclosing Party's interests. Now the section states that the receiver agrees to contact the disclosing party. This internal contradiction will create enforcement uncertainty on what really are the communication expectations between the parties and whether they can contact each other, which is a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 7. Communications to Disclosing Party ; Notification . The Receiving Party agrees to initiate or maintain contact (except for those contacts made in the ordinary course of business) with any officer, director, manager, partner , member , shareholder, employee or agent of the Disclosing Party regarding its business, operations, prospects or finances, except with the express permission of the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 . It is understood that the Representative (s) of the Disclosing Party identified in Section 10 will arrange for appropriate contacts for due diligence purposes. Each Party agrees to give the other Party prompt notice of any violation of this A greement, including, without limitation, any unauthorized disclosure or use of the other Party’s Confidential Information .
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 7 stated that the Receiving Party agrees 'not to initiate' contact with the Disclosing Party's personnel. The changed text removes 'not,' so now Section 7 states that the Receiving Party agrees 'to initiate' contact. This creates a direct in-text contradiction within Section 7 itself (since the contradicted text is actually Section 7 itself but in the changed version, so in essence there is an in-text contradiction) because the section now both requires and restricts contact. This leads to ambiguity and uncertainty regarding communication expectations, which aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 11. Remedies . It is further understood and agreed that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a Party or any of its Representatives and that the other Party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach (without the need to post any bond in connection therewith) . Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by a Party of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all oth er remedies available at law or equity to such Party. Each Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party and its officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives forever harmless from an d against any and all losses actually sustained or incurred by the Disclosing Party relating to, resulting from or otherwise arising from a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 11. Remedies . It is further understood and agreed that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a Party or any of its Representatives and that the other Party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach (without the need to post any bond in connection therewith) . Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by a Party of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all oth er remedies available at law or equity to such Party. Each Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party and its officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives forever harmless from an d against any and all losses actually sustained or incurred by the Disclosing Party relating to, resulting from or otherwise arising from a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Section 11 states that money damages would *not* be a sufficient remedy. The perturbation changes this to state that money damages *would* be a sufficient remedy. This creates a direct in-text contradiction. The original text said money damages are not sufficient, however, since the perturbation, the first line now states the opposite of what it stated previously regarding the same text, this internal contradiction will create enforcement uncertainty on what really are the remedies for any breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 11. Remedies . It is further understood and agreed that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by a Party or any of its Representatives and that the other Party shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach (without the need to post any bond in connection therewith) . Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach by a Party of this Agreement but shall be in addition to all oth er remedies available at law or equity to such Party. Each Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party and its officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives forever harmless from an d against any and all losses actually sustained or incurred by the Disclosing Party relating to, resulting from or otherwise arising from a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation changes "money damages would not be a sufficient remedy" to "money damages would be a sufficient remedy" within Section 11. The Contradicted Text is located in the same section as the Changed Text. Since the original section 11 stated money damages are not sufficient, however, since the perturbation, the first line now states the opposite of what it stated previously regarding the same text. Thus, this creates a direct and strong in-text contradiction as per the explanation.
